case_name: Methiocarb removal from contaminated well water for potable supply
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for removing methiocarb from well water intended for drinking water use under moderate cost and conventional operating constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A small town operates production wells near agricultural land and has detected methiocarb in one well used for public drinking water supply. The utility wants a proven treatment approach that can be operated by conventional staff without introducing high energy demand. Raw water is otherwise typical of groundwater, but moderate natural organic matter and hardness may influence adsorptive performance and media replacement frequency.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable reduction of methiocarb to a level consistent with drinking water protection goals and maintain stable potable water quality.
secondary_goals: use established treatment technology for pesticide control

core_unit_functions.pre_filtration: remove incidental particulates and protect downstream adsorptive media
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb methiocarb and related hydrophobic organic micropollutants from groundwater
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain carbon fines and polish finished water
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide microbiological protection for distributed drinking water

applicability_rationale: methiocarb is an organic pesticide for which activated carbon is a well-established control approach in drinking water treatment
groundwater has low turbidity, making a compact adsorption-based train practical and robust
moderate DOC indicates matrix competition is present but still compatible with GAC operation when bed life is monitored
this option fits a utility preference for conventional and proven treatment without high-pressure systems
potential_risks: natural organic matter can shorten GAC run length and increase replacement frequency
insufficient empty bed contact time may cause early breakthrough
performance depends on routine monitoring for pesticide breakthrough and timely media changeout

core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_dosing: adsorb dissolved methiocarb prior to solids separation
core_unit_functions.coagulation: capture PAC and associated contaminants into settleable or filterable flocs
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove formed solids and PAC carryover
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: PAC is a recognized option for episodic or moderate organic micropollutant removal
suitable where the utility can handle chemical dosing and filtration using conventional practices
provides operational flexibility if pesticide occurrence varies seasonally
can be effective for carbamate pesticide control when dosing is adjusted to water quality
potential_risks: treatment efficiency is sensitive to PAC dose, contact time, and DOC competition
creates additional solids residuals and requires reliable chemical feed control
less stable than fixed-bed GAC for continuous long-term removal

core_unit_functions.pre_filtration: protect oxidation equipment and downstream media from particulate fouling
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation: transform methiocarb through hydroxyl-radical based oxidation
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_carbon_or_gac_polish: remove oxidation byproducts and residual organics

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can degrade pesticide contaminants when adsorption alone is insufficient or when additional polishing is desired
post-carbon polishing is appropriate because oxidation may form intermediate compounds that still require removal
technically strong for organic micropollutants but more complex than straightforward carbon adsorption
may be considered where treatment goals emphasize added resilience against variable pesticide contamination
potential_risks: oxidation conditions must be optimized to avoid incomplete transformation
higher energy and control demands may exceed the preference for conventional operation
requires management of oxidant safety and potential byproduct formation

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: reject dissolved organic contaminants including many pesticide molecules
core_unit_functions.stabilization: restore finished water chemistry and corrosion stability after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: membranes can provide strong removal of a broad range of micropollutants
this option may be attractive only if multiple additional dissolved contaminants also require control
technically feasible but not the most proportionate solution for a single primary pesticide issue in otherwise typical well water
potential_risks: concentrate disposal is a major burden for a small groundwater utility
membrane fouling control and post-treatment increase operational complexity
high energy use is difficult to justify for this relatively straightforward case

core_unit_functions.air_stripping_or_simple_aeration: attempt removal of volatile compounds and improve aeration

applicability_rationale: included as a low-ranking comparator rather than a preferred solution
simple aeration is conventional but not well matched to methiocarb, which is not typically treated by volatility-based removal
this train does not address the primary contaminant using the most relevant mechanism
potential_risks: may create false confidence because conventional simplicity does not equal contaminant applicability
finished water may remain noncompliant with pesticide control objectives
